description_tesim: Crum was an avid collector of family memorabilia and other items, many of which were photographed by Adelbert Bartlett. Two objects appeared in newspaper articles: a wreath created by poet Ina Donna Coolbrith, and a spur gifted to the Dalton family by Don Augustin Vincente Zamorano, executive secretary to Governor Jose Maria Echeandia. See image ark nos. 21198/zz002d3x0f,  21198/zz002d3wvw, 21198/zz002d5dcs, and 21198/zz002d7802.

Copy print portrait of John H. (John Henninger) Crum of Santa Monica. Crum was a 3rd generation Dalton family descendant (a California pioneer family), being the nephew of W.H. Perry and Elizabeth Dalton Perry. He was also a member of the Santa Monica-Ocean Park Chamber of Commerce.

title_tesim: Adult students in Mr. Cagle's photography class, University High School, 1965
caption_tesim: Mr. Cagle photo class, 1965, Unihi.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of adult students in a photography class. The students are seated at desks, some viewed holding cameras or with cameras placed on the desks before them. Additional students stand in the back of the class. Most of the students' attentions are directed toward a man standing next to a blackboard (presumably the instructor, Mr. Cagle). On the blackboard are driving directions the Watts Towers. Text reads: "2.00 PM... Watts Towers. Harbor Freeway. Left on Century. Right on Central 3 blocks. Left on 103rd. Right on Wilmington..." Photographed at University High School, West Los Angeles. This is a science classroom with gas outlets for bunsen burners on the desks and physics equations on the back wall.

title_tesim: May Betteridge in a garden with large terracotta pots, Los Angeles, 1928-1934
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2663 P1 to 4. May Betteridge. Various. Myrtle ave. near Pico 1300 Blk.
Handwritten on negative: 2663 P2

description_tesim: May Betteridge seated on a lawn in a garden looking towards a display of large terracotta pots. Five pots are lined up with 2 of them on a garden bench with lion figurine supports. Behind her are more pots and pieces of garden sculpture depicting mythological subjects including a putto with a fish (left) and a seated nude or Aphrodite figure. The garden is enclosed by tall bushes and cypress trees.
May Betteridge appears in Los Angeles Times reportage from 1929 to 1934 and again in 1949-1950. She was known for her "little-theater" work by 1928 and was the National Orange Show Queen in 1934.

title_tesim: Santa Monica's host city float in the California Admission Day parade, Santa Monica, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2847. Admission Day 1937. Santa Monica Parade. Bill Hart Jr, Irvin S Cobb, Leo Carrillo.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The parade route began on 4th St. at Colorado Ave., followed north along 4th St. to Wilshire Blvd., and turned westward toward Ocean Ave. where it moved south. The procession passed the review stand at Ocean and Broadway before ending at Pico Blvd.

This float appears to have had two reproductions of the California State Seal with two living representations of “Minerva” seated on opposite sides. For alternate views of this float, see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d7f52 and 21198/zz002d7g3h.

Santa Monica’s host city float in the California Admission Day parade. Spectators line both sides of 4th St. as the float passes in front of Security First National Bank (at right). At the center of the float is a reproduction of the Seal of California, including a live model posed as the Roman goddess Minerva, seated and holding a staff with a shield propped near her feet. The seal’s other symbolic emblems – a California grizzly bear feeding on a cluster of grapes, a sheaf of wheat, a miner with pickax, and ships sailing in the bay – are all represented here. The phrase “Eureka” is printed below thirty-one stars, above the mountains and rising sun. Surrounding the image are the words “The Great Seal of the State of California,” replicating the version of the state seal that was standardized in 1937. To the left of the shield is a small statue of a Franciscan monk and a model of a sailing ship. Two additional monks are viewed on a small ledge at right, below a rendering of a contemporary view of the Santa Monica skyline above what appears to be cliffs. Words along the side of the platform read: “Santa Monica – California’s Host 1937… Santa Monica.” Photographed on 4th St. at Santa Monica Blvd. Businesses following the parade route north include: Security First National Bank; [second floor above the bank:] “F.R. Lee Insurance,” and “Law Offices Nelson D. Miller.” “Steams[hip] Pacific…Dollar…,” “Motor Coach Stage,” “The Ann Howe Shop,” “The Model Barber

In 1937, Santa Monica hosted the California Admission Day celebration and convention of the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West. The three-day event (held September 8-10), commemorated the 87th anniversary of California’s admission to the Union. The Los Angeles Times published several articles between September 7th and 10th that described planned festivities, but a more detailed account of the celebration can be found in The Grizzly Bear, a magazine published by the Native Sons and Daughters of the Golden West (1905-1954). The article is titled, "Happy Memories of Admission Day," The Grizzly Bear, 1 Oct. 1937: 3.

Santa Monica City Hall was located at 4th Street and Oregon Avenue (later changed to Santa Monica Boulevard) from 1903 to 1938.

Participants in the California Admission Day celebration and parade, photographed on the steps of City Hall. Identified participants include: Postmaster Philip T. Hill (far left), Mayor Edmond Gillette (seen at center wearing a cowboy hat, boots, a sheriff’s badge, and a gun belt with two guns); columnist Irvin S. Cobb stands to his right. The young boy standing in front of Cobb wears a cowboy hat, neckerchief, wrist cuffs, and cowhide chaps.
The child is photographed in two additional images (see image ark nos. 21198/zz002d7f6k and 21198/zz002d7ff6).

title_tesim: Procession of Army artillery trucks and gunners at the Armistice Day parade, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2876. Armistice Day 1938, Santa Monica, also Soldiers Home. Col. RA Bringham Mgr, John F. Eaton, Veteran (age 92) of John A Martin Post GAR. Parade of Santa Monica Streets.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: A procession of Army artillery trucks mounted with air defense artillery and carrying gunners, photographed during the Santa Monica Armistice Day parade. The gunners wear dress uniforms and helmets with badges. The men are part of the California Army National Guard, 115th Quartermaster Regiment. Identifying information is painted on each truck, though only the two trucks in the foreground carry legible information. Printed on a plate attached to the truck at left: "115th QM Regt. No...." [Printed on the hood:] "U.S.A. W-38124." [Text surrounding an emblem printed on the driver-side door:] "115 CAL NG." The truck at right provides similar information. Text printed on the plate reads: "115th QM Regt. No. 31" [Printed on the hood:] "U.S.A. W-38151." The text and emblem found on the passenger-side door are the same as the truck on the left. This is the Quartermaster Corps' branch emblem - comprised of an eagle with wings spread, perched on a wheel set with 13 stars. Superimposed on the wheel are a crossed sword and skeleton key.

Santa Monica's Armistice Day parade is noted in the article, "Southland Cities Ready for Patriotic Services: Parade, School Pageants and Assemblies to Feature Armistice Observances Today," Los Angeles Times, 11 Nov. 1938.

title_tesim: Flag bearers marching in the Armistice Day parade, Santa Monica, 1938

description_tesim: Santa Monica's Armistice Day parade is noted in the article, "Southland Cities Ready for Patriotic Services: Parade, School Pageants and Assemblies to Feature Armistice Observances Today," Los Angeles Times, 11 Nov. 1938.

Photograph of Veterans serving as color guards in the Santa Monica Armistice Day parade. Spectators watch from the sidelines as eight men pass carrying bayonets on their right shoulders (one of the men is obscured by a soldier seen at foreground). The men wear a variety of uniforms, some reminiscent of Spanish-American War regalia, including campaign hats, canvas leggings and cartridge belts around their waists. The remaining men wear garrison caps with emblems and (possibly) the names of their posts embroidered on the sides (as with the men at center who also wear "Sam Browne" belts - a wide belt with a narrow strap across the chest. The soldiers are followed by a number of uniformed men bearing the American flag.

title_tesim: Wheat fields, San Luis Obispo County
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2865. Wheat Field. San Luis Obispo County.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Wheat production in San Luis Obispo County can be traced to the Mission San Luis Obispo in the 1770s. It was in the Santa Margarita Valley that the Mission built an outlying farm that the fathers named “Asistencia de Santa Margarita.” The farm’s wine-grape vineyards and wheat fields were farmed by the Mission Chumash. As farmers began to procure former mission lands, wheat farming spread throughout the county. Areas included (but were not limited to) the Arroyo Grande, San Juan, Estrella, Edna, and Santa Margarita valleys. The Gold Rush of the 1840s provided a boon for local farmers who produced the grains and goods needed for the area’s growing population. Grain production continued after the population boom, with San Luis Obispo becoming a leader in wheat production between the years 1880-1890. Though the bulk of grain production eventually shifted to the Mid-western U.S., San Luis Obispo County continues to produce grains on a small scale along with other agricultural products.
Photograph of wheat fields in San Luis Obispo County. The photo captures rolling hills dotted by trees, viewed here at left and in the distance.

title_tesim: Wheat fields, San Luis Obispo County
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2865. Wheat Field. San Luis Obispo County.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of wheat fields in San Luis Obispo County. The photo captures a tree at foreground and rolling hills in the background.

Wheat production in San Luis Obispo County can be traced to the Mission San Luis Obispo in the 1770s. It was in the Santa Margarita Valley that the Mission built an outlying farm that the fathers named “Asistencia de Santa Margarita.” The farm’s wine-grape vineyards and wheat fields were farmed by the Mission Chumash. As farmers began to procure former mission lands, wheat farming spread throughout the county. Areas included (but were not limited to) the Arroyo Grande, San Juan, Estrella, Edna, and Santa Margarita valleys. The Gold Rush of the 1840s provided a boon for local farmers who produced the grains and goods needed for the area’s growing population. Grain production continued after the population boom, with San Luis Obispo becoming a leader in wheat production between the years 1880-1890. Though the bulk of grain production eventually shifted to the Mid-western U.S., San Luis Obispo County continues to produce grains on a small scale along with other agricultural products.

title_tesim: Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War in the California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic parade, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2875. Veterans Parade, Santa Monica, 1938.
named_subject_tesim: Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War
Grand Army of the Republic. Department of California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of the Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War, Department of California and Nevada, in the annual California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic parade. They are led by two women bearing American flags. One wears a kerchief on her head and holds an embroidered bag. They are followed by a woman carrying the Nevada flag (the slogan "Battle Born" can be seen above the star in the left corner). A woman carrying a California flag is viewed at right. A third woman carries a banner behind them, faintly imprinted with the name John W. Grimes (not visible in this photo). A succession of American flags follow along with the women wearing white skirts or dresses, jackets and hats. The procession follows Wilshire Boulevard and is captured nearing 3rd Street. Automobiles are parked along the commercial thoroughfare in front of a two-story building with a variety of stores on the ground floor. Signs on the building and printed on awnings read: "Medical Building," “Pan -Pan Studios, 318 Gifts…Tea Shop 319,” “The D.B Wright Investment… Real Estate Insurance Rentals… Property Management 321,”“Marinello” “Health Foods… 325 Santa Monica Health Food Co.,” “Medical Building 327; “…Shoppe” and “Templeman’s.” An additional store front (name illegible) sits next to a corner space drug store corner. A Texaco gas station and its sign are viewed next to the Leon Apartments.

The parade was part of the 71st annual encampment of the Grand Army of the Republic, Department of California and Nevada was held in Santa Monica, May 1-6, 1938. The event was noted in the article, “All but Two Ride in Parade: Pair of Nonagenarian G.A.R. Veterans Still Able to March Afoot,” Los Angeles Times, 5 May 1938: A2
An additional photograph provides a clearer view of the flag imprinted with the name John W. Grimes (image ark no. J21198/zz002d7tsp).

The Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War was founded in 1885 as an organization dedicated to memorializing Civil War veterans. The organization is an auxiliary of the Grand Army of the Republic (G.A.R.). In 1938, the Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War, Department of California and Nevada, dedicated a plaque to John W Grimes (Department Commander, Grand Army of the Republic), located in a garden at Palisades Park. That same year, a second plaque was dedicated at Long Beach Public Library by the Sons and Daughters of the Union Veterans of Civil War. Grimes was associated with G.A.R. Long Beach Post No. 181.
combined_subject_ssim: Daughters of Union Veterans of the Civil War
Grand Army of the Republic. Department of California

description_tesim: Photograph of a youth bugle corps marching in the annual California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic parade. The boys, who range in age from approximately twelve to eighteen years, wear military dress uniforms, military hats with insignias, epaulets, and medals. They are followed by a two groups of similarly dressed boys, each group with standard bearers carrying the American flag and (presumably) their troop or organization's flag. The military groups are immediately followed by a Boy Scout troop, with additional groups seen extending into the distance. The procession follows Wilshire Boulevard and is captured here at 3rd Street. Signs on the buildings and awnings read: “…Shoes…Ultimate in Foot Comfort,” “…Shoppe 309” “Millinery Dressmaking…Silk, Yardage, Woolen,” “Maher Music Co.,” “Wilshire Cafeteria,” “Pan-Pan Studios, 318 Gifts…Tea Shop 319,” “The D.B Wright Investment… Real Estate Insurance Rentals… Property Management 321,”“Marinello” “Health Foods… 325 Santa Monica Health Food Co.,” “Medical Building 327; “…Shoppe” and “Templeman’s.” An additional store front (name illegible) sits next to a corner space drug store. Finally, a Texaco gas station and its sign are viewed next to the Leon Apartments.

description_tesim: Photograph of young boys in dress uniform marching in the annual California-Nevada Department, Grand Army of the Republic parade. The boys are likely from one of the military academies that marched in the parade. A flag carried by a standard bearer (out of view) flies overhead (appears to be imprinted with crossing sabers and "ICO" printed beneath them). The military group is followed by a Boy Scout troop that includes two flag bearers - one holding an American flag, the other holding a flag that reads, "Cres[cent Bay...] Boy Scouts of [America] Santa Monica." Additional troops can be seen in the extreme distance. The procession follows Wilshire Boulevard and is captured  here at 3rd Street. Signs on the buildings and awnings read: “Ernest Stone Optometrist” “…Shoes…Ultimate in Foot Comfort,” “…Shoppe 309” “Millinery Dressmaking…Silk, Yardage, Woolen,” “Maher Music Co.,” “Wilshire Cafeteria”  amongst others.

The parade was part of the 71st annual encampment of the Grand Army of the Republic, Department of California and Nevada was held in Santa Monica, May 1-6, 1938. description_tesim: The dedication ceremony was part of the 71st annual encampment of the Grand Army of the Republic, Department of California and Nevada, held May 1-6 in Santa Monica. The event was noted in the article, “G.A.R. to Open Meeting Today: Delegates Will be Registered in Hotel Miramar at Beach,” Los Angeles Times, 1 May 1938: 10

Members of the Woman's Relief Corps and additional participants at a tree dedication ceremony at the Sawtelle Veterans Home. A woman stands at the fore of the group holding a small box and reading from an open program or leaflet. Six women wearing light-colored attire stand behind her, with five of the six women bearing flags over their right shoulders. Each woman wears a badge on her chest (some are Woman's Relief Corps badges with the characteristic Maltese cross attached to a ribbon and bar pin). The group is joined by three men and a woman seen in the background. The photo may have been taken on Veterans Day, November 11, 1938.
A second photograph of the same ceremony provides additional information (image ark no. 21198/zz002d7tr5).

title_tesim: Mayor Claude C. Crawford and participants in an Army draft ceremony, Santa Monica, 1940
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2921. SMonica City Hall. Army Draft Ceremony, City Hall Steps, Santa Monica, 11/22/40. Mayor Claude C. Crawford. 1st draft World War II.
named_subject_tesim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of Mayor Claude C. Crawford and ten conscripted men at an Army draft ceremony. The men were some of the first to be conscripted under the Selective Service Act. They stand with several ceremony participants on the steps of Santa Monica City Hall. Some of the participants hold their right hands over their chests. A policeman (seen in the distance at left) and others in military uniform are saluting. The American flag, Santa Monica Bay Cities American Legion flag, and Veteran's Service League flag are also on view.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of California State Guardsmen (California State Military Reserve) in uniform on the steps of Santa Monica City Hall. The men range in age from about 18 to approximately 50 years old. They are standing at ease with rifles at their sides, rifle butt on ground. A Captain stands at foreground, while a Sargent (three stripes up) and Staff Sargent (three stripes up, one down)stand behind him at left. A fringed banner with the American colors is partially viewed hanging over the building's entrance.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Concert at Symphonies by the Sea at the Memorial Greek Amphitheatre, Santa Monica, 1939-1945

description_tesim: View from audience seats of a concert during Santa Monica's annual cultural event, "Symphonies by the Sea" at the Open-Air Memorial Theater on the Santa Monica High School campus. A conductor in the foreground conducts a musical ensemble of violinists, clarinet player, trumpeteers, cellists, flautists, a pianist and singers.  Symphonies by the Sea was a free-admission annual symphonic and operatic season which started at Santa Monica High School, then moved on to Santa Monica City College.
combined_subject_ssim: Symphonies by the Sea (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Santa Monica City Hall under construction, Santa Monica, 1938
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2926. Santa Monica, New City Hall Building itself. 12/1/39. "Dedication" [struck out].
named_subject_tesim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This is a picture of the historic Santa Monica City Hall while it was under construction. The building is still surrounded by scaffolding, and two workers can be seen painting the facade in the foreground of the picture. The words "Cuty Hall" have already been etched into the entrance of the building. Santa Monica City Hall's period of significance was considered to be from 1938-1951. It was built in the famous depression-era style, PWA (Public Works Administration) Moderne, which was a combination of the Art deco and Art mdoerne styles of architecture. This type of architecture was most often used in buildings financed by the federal government.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)

title_tesim: Santa Monica City Hall, Santa Monica, 1939

description_tesim: This is a view of the historic Santa Monica City Hall looking south-east from beyond the low fences surrounding it. The driveway is lined with trees and gardens. Cars are parked next to the building to the left of the image. Santa Monica City Hall is considered one of the best examples of Public Works Administration (PWA) Moderne architecture, a style popularized by depression-era architects. This style is a combination of the Art deco and Art moderne architecture styles, most often found in buildings funded by the federal government.
combined_subject_ssim: Santa Monica City Hall (Santa Monica, Calif.)

The City Hall is considered a classic example of the Public Works Administration (PWA) Moderne style of architecture, made popular by depression-era architects. This was a combination of Art deco and Art moderne styles, and is often found in buildings financed by the federal government.

title_tesim: Man diving into El Mirador hotel pool, Palm Springs, 1941
caption_tesim: Text on negative sleeve: No. 2761-P410-421, El Mirador Hotel, Palm Springs, Pool - 2/1/41
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: This is a picture taken at the historic El Mirador Hotel in Palm Springs, regarded one of the finest luxury hotels of its time. This is a picture of a diver diving into its famous Olympic-size swimming pool that had five diving boards, an underwater window on the pool for use by photographers, and a pool observation platform. In the background, we can see the most defining feature of the hotel, a pink Byzantine tower from which the hotel took its name — El Mirador — meaning "The Lookout."

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Carolyn's Negatives. Please En[close...] group lab... Please contact...group 2. Also contact (all) & enlarge... [on verso:] up (last night). [Sleeve is an envelope torn down the length of one side. Portions of text missing.]

repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Date range derived from a second negative sleeve housing visually and thematically similar images. The sleeve is an envelope post marked "Apr 25, 1937". In addition, both sleeves contained photographs of a newly built Santa Monica High School structure. One photograph is of a campus construction site. The photograph documents post-earthquake reconstruction that fell under the Works Project Administration (WPA). The project was completed in 1939. (For construction photo, see image ark no. 21198/zz002d8j8h)

Photograph of a still life set-up, perhaps for art class instruction, comprised of a short, bowl-shaped vase holding daffodils. Three daffodil stems are arranged at an angle near the vase atop a bunched portion of cloth. A stripped-edge textile is draped over a two-panel divider that acts as a background element. Photographed by Carolyn Bartlett, Santa Monica High School.
Composition variation, see image ark no. 21198/zz002d8j91.

(For construction photo, see image ark no. 21198/zz002d8j8h)

Photograph of children paticipating in the Novelties & Comics event in the Seventeenth Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade. Two girls dressed in feathered hats and checked aprons hold a sign, "Comics - Walking & Vehicles" in front of a small car that seats a young girl and boy dressed in a plaid suit. Two girls dressed similar to the girls in front follow the car, which has "[M/H]iller Special" printed on the side. Two women and a man stand behind the children, possible parents. A tortoise crawls off the frame at the bottom right, while te sign for "St. Regis Apt." can be seen in the background.

The Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade was community event started in 1919. The 1936 parade was organized by the Santa Monica Bay clubwomen and civic leaders with Mayor Gillette and the city council lending their support. Flowers were provided by City parks and private gardens.

title_tesim: Fancy Dress participants at the Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade, Santa Monica, 1936
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2927, Ocean Park, Children's Parade, 1936
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Fancy Dress participants at the Seventeenth Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade, dressed as people from different parts of the country. A young girl holds a sign "Fancy Dress," while the girl next to her holds up, "Immigrants 'All' Americans 'All'" and the boy after holds up, "Ve Missed the Mayflower." The girl in the center holds an accordion which has Shirley written on it. The young girl next, wearing a hat of posies and holding up an umbrella covered with posies, carries a basket with the sign, "Posies, 5c." Following are children in a Hawaiian costume, Statue of Liberty costume and a Polka-Dotted dress with a sign ,"God Bless America, Feels great to be an American."
The event was covered in a Los Angeles Times article, "Hundreds of Children to be in Floral Parade," 27 Aug. 1936: 10.

The Annual Ocean Park Children's Floral Parade was a community event started in 1919. The 1936 parade was organized by the Santa Monica Bay clubwomen and civic leaders with Mayor Gillette and the city council also lending their support. Flowers were provided by city parks and private gardens.

description_tesim: The Children's Home Society of California is a non-profit child welfare agency founded in 1891. It became the first licensed child placement agency in California in 1911. It was located at 919 E. 25th St. from the 1920's up to 1942, when the organization moved to the Ozro W. Childs mansion at 3100 W. Adams Blvd. where it remained until 1977.
Photograph of a boy feeding a goat a carrot at the Children's Home Society. The boy is seated porch. A briefcase sits next to the door behind him.

title_tesim: Fountain and statue, Mission San Fernando Rey de España, Los Angeles, 1929
caption_tesim: Handwritten at edge of negative: 2015

Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2011 - 2012 - 2013 San Fernando.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  San Fernando Mission, Calif., on old El Camino Real (King's Highway) running the length of the state.  San Fernando Mission, dating from 1797, erected by Spanish padres, now Los Angeles city park, 25 mi. north of city proper, popular tourist attraction.  Contains museum.  In days of padres this mission was a spiritual and welfare center of a large area.  1 view of mission building, 1 view of arcades looking out

title_tesim: Fountain, Mission San Fernando Rey de España, Los Angeles, 1929
caption_tesim: Text from nitrate negative sleeve: 2010, San Fernando Mission, 1929.  Photo by Adelbert Bartlett, 535 15th Street, Santa Monica, Calif.  San Fernando Mission Fountain at this mission, replica of one at Cordova, Spain, erected, about 1812-14, by early Spanish mission padres; 100 years later mission grounds donated to city of Los Angeles as public park.  Mission is at head of San Fernando Valley, 25 mi. north of L.A. city center.  Los Angeles acquaduct water, coming from Sierra Mts. 250 mi. away runs into this fountain.

title_tesim: Photograph of newspaper article, Baldwin Gems Sold to Broker, 1930
caption_tesim: Handwritten on clipping: 12/6/30, 12/7/1930
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Text reads: Baldwin Gems Sold to Broker.  Million-Dollar Family Jewels of Clara Stocker Dispersed by Executors.  New York, Dec. 6 (Exclusive)--Harry Winston, Fifth-avenue diamond broker, today announced the purchase of the jewels of the late Mrs. Clara Baldwin Stocker, daughter of E. J. (Lucky) Baldwin, pioneer California mining operator, gambler and sportsman.  The collection is reported to be valued at approximately $1,000,000.  The purchase was made from Albert E. Snyder and Mrs. Rosebud Mullender, son and daughter of Mrs. Stocker and executors of the estate.  The jewelry included hundreds of stones, totaling more than 1000 carats.  There are tiaras, necklaces, rings, earrings, festoons, pendants and vanity cases.  The jewels will arrive here on Tuesday, Mr. Winston said.

description_tesim: Digitization took place as part of the California Cultures Project.

Rancho San Jose comprised what is now the present day city of Pomona and environs.  Rancho Azusa de Dalton (not to be confused with Rancho Azusa de Duarte to the west), extended from as far east as what is now Pomona and as far west as what is now Arcadia.

title_tesim: Inglewood the hub city : map of Los Angeles County, Southwest
dimensions_tesim: 51 x 45 cm. originally folded to 25 x 12 cm.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: The map on side 1 shows Inglewood in the middle of the greater Los Angeles area, with red lines emanating from that "hub" showing mileage from Inglewood to specified destinations. The other side of the map contains promotional material about Inglewood (including that "Inglewood is composed of Caucasians only"), including descriptions of its attractions (with caption title, Desirable residence conditions), a city business directory and a short recap of this information. The entire sheet is intended to be folded and used as a brochure for real estate purposes.
Digitization took place as part of the California Cultures Project.

title_tesim: At evening I sketched this unusual cloud formation, 1942 May 31.
uniform_title_tesim: At evening I sketched this beautiful, unusual cloud formation. All of us noticed it. We wondered whether these might mean that we would be at peace.

title_tesim: Did you ever see such individual dress attire?, n.d.
caption_tesim: "Did you ever see such individual dress attire? This is a high school gym class. I ran into on the dusty field next to block 7. Facing each other in circle, they would clap their hands, say somet[h]ing, laugh. Off in the corner another class came dashing by in a cloud of dust. But school is wonderful." Unsigned
